project_overview.project_name: Second Greater Beirut Water Supply Project (SGBWSP)
project_overview.project_development_objective: To improve the water supply services in the Greater Beirut and Mount Lebanon area
project_overview.beneficiaries: 1.8 million people in Greater Beirut and Mount Lebanon area, including residents in Beirut Southern Suburbs (Zone A), Damour-Kfashima (Zone C), and Tallet El Khayat (Zone TA), plus 650,000 residents in Greater Beirut Area and Coastal Metn for NRW reduction benefits

geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - Lebanon is experiencing multi-faceted crisis including financial collapse since 2019, political instability with no president since October 2022, and escalation of Middle East conflict in 2024 causing infrastructure damage, displacement of hundreds of thousands, and severe disruption of essential services and government operations

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Completing remaining bulk water infrastructure
key_components.description: Completion of bulk water infrastructure including tunnel lining (24km), twin transmission pipelines (1.6km), Joun Regulation Structure, regional reservoirs (Hadath 125, Hadath 90, Hazmieh 90) with total storage capacity of 120,000m3, and construction supervision services. Includes rehabilitation of potentially damaged infrastructure from conflict.

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Strengthening treatment processes for improved water quality
key_components.description: Completion of Wardanieh Water Treatment Plant construction, installation of add-ons (Powder Activated Carbon system) through adaptive management approach, daily raw water quality monitoring and early warning system, laboratory upgrades at Dbayeh, enhanced chlorination practices, and repairs to distribution network infrastructure damaged by conflict.

key_components.component_name: Component 3: Reforms for improved utility operations
key_components.description: NRW reduction in four zones (Baabda Suburbs, Coastal Metn, Tallet El Khayat, Bourj Abu Hadar) through customer census, DMA establishment, installation of 40,000 household smart meters; digitalization of BMLWE systems including SCADA rehabilitation with solar panels, ERP customization, asset inventory, billing/collection digitalization; communications strategy and public awareness campaigns; capacity building for women in technical positions.

key_components.component_name: Component 4: Land acquisition and resettlement compensation
key_components.description: Land acquisition and resettlement compensation for displaced persons for reservoirs (Hadath 90, Hazmieh 90), twin transmission pipelines section, Joun Regulation Structure, and Wardanieh WTP outflow, following Resettlement Action Plan and addendum provisions.

key_components.component_name: Component 5: Project management and capacity building
key_components.description: Operation of Project Management Units at CDR and BMLWE, MoEW oversight support, knowledge exchange activities, apprenticeship and certification program targeting women for technical positions (target 50% female participation, 60 apprentices total), capacity building for women in technical roles, and institutional strengthening.
Zero allocation at project onset, triggered only in emergency circumstances to allow flexible response to crises.

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Ministry of Energy and Water (MoEW) with delegation to Council for Development and Reconstruction (CDR) for Components 1 and 4, and Beirut Mount Lebanon Water Establishment (BMLWE) for Components 2 and 3, with both for Component 5
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Management Units (PMUs) at CDR and BMLWE headed by Project Coordinators with procurement officers, financial management officers, engineers, environmental/health/safety specialist, and social specialist shared between both PMUs. Cooperation Agreement between BMLWE and Litani River Authority (LRA) for water allocation and operational arrangements. Coordination with development partners including AFD, EU, AICS, GiZ, USAID, IsDB, AFESD, UN agencies, and NGOs. Dam Safety Panel of Experts (DSPOE) for dam safety oversight.
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF (September 2023), use of Bank Standard Procurement Documents, transaction-based disbursement, three disbursement categories with separate Designated Accounts, direct payment method available for both CDR and BMLWE, advance procurement authorized, STEP platform required for all contracts, forced labor declarations required for solar PV panels and other materials
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Integrated M&E system with PMUs responsible for data collection on performance indicators and progress reporting. Contractors report works-specific intermediate indicators verified by construction supervision firms. PMU engineers oversee data collection, analysis, and evaluation with monthly consolidated reports. Social specialist reports on gender and citizen engagement. Quarterly progress reports submitted to Bank. Midterm review planned to assess progress and potential adjustments. M&E framework integrated into Project Operations Manual.

identified_risks.risk_description: Lebanon's Parliament has not selected a new President since October 30, 2022, and government continues in caretaker capacity. Escalation of Middle East conflict in 2024 has impacted implementation pace. Protracted financial and economic crisis has caused implementation delays due to government's inability to finance contracts, constraints in land expropriation, and procurement delays. Signing of Loan Agreement and Parliamentary ratification may be delayed due to conflict.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Strong government ownership from MoEW, BMLWE, and CDR with demonstrated commitment to completing project. Close follow-up with Government and continued dialogue at every level. Planning implementation timing to account for prolonged period between approval and effectiveness.

identified_risks.risk_description: Lebanon experienced severe economic contraction of nearly 35% since 2019, erasing 15 years of growth. Lebanese pound lost over 98% of value with triple-digit inflation through 2023. Banking system collapse resulted in dollarized cash-based economy. Conflict in Middle East further shocked economy with negative impacts continuing into 2024. Rising construction costs reduce bidder interest and increase procurement risks. Inflation surges remain risk due to conflict.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Contingencies included in major contracts denominated in US dollars or euros to manage currency crisis effects. Lebanese pound stabilized at 89,500 LBP/US$ since July 2023, slowing inflation to double digits by 2024.

identified_risks.risk_description: Current challenging context has significantly impacted CDR and BMLWE, forcing allocation of limited human and financial resources to emergency response and service continuity. If crisis continues throughout implementation, could severely threaten institutional capacity of both agencies, straining limited resources and reducing ability to absorb Project demands. High employee turnover in public sector due to economic conditions.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Reinforcing both PMUs with adequate staff including additional engineer to BMLWE PMU. Continuous capacity building and engagement of BMLWE staff through Project activities. Ongoing support and supervision from World Bank. Hands-on Expanded Implementation Support (HEIS) may be extended upon request.

identified_risks.risk_description: High procurement risk due to challenging environment, limited availability of civil servants, currency deterioration, lengthy procurement transactions, dysfunctional banking sector, and limited access to guarantees/loans for contractor liquidity. High FM risk due to widespread corruption perception, currency devaluation, lack of trust, high employee turnover, coordination complexity between two agencies, unresolved lapsed loan affecting Designated Accounts, resource limitations, and 2022 Entity audit of BMLWE revealed financial management deficiencies. Country's Court of Accounts lacks human resources to audit project.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Hiring dedicated financial officers. Establishing segregated Designated Accounts in US$ to preserve fund value. Direct payments available for both CDR and BMLWE. Using existing accounting systems. Enhanced financial reporting through quarterly interim reports and external audits. Recruitment of consultancy firm to audit fixed assets and support account adjustments. ERP system customization to include fixed assets registration module. Preparation of operations manual with financial management guidelines. Establishment of separate funds flow mechanism for resettlement compensation. Use of Bank Standard Procurement Documents and complaints mechanism. Both agencies have PMU experience from GBWSP. Advance procurement authorized to speed implementation.

identified_risks.risk_description: Feasibility of constructing Hadath 90 and Hazmieh 90 reservoirs depends on successful land expropriation, which proved challenging in previous water sector operations in Lebanon. Monthly water quality monitoring at Joun reservoir intake shows high ammonia peaks during dry season beyond current planned treatment capacity. Risk that daily monitoring will show higher pollution levels than expected, requiring higher investment for adequate treatment. Uncertainty in optimal WTP add-ons configuration.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Design of supply system allowing operation with one, two, or three regional reservoirs depending on expropriation progress. Adequate provision of funds for treatment adjustments allowing design flexibility. Daily monitoring of raw water quality. Coordination with LRA and other IFIs to reduce pollution at source. Adaptive management approach for WTP with 'no regrets' investments (PAC system, daily monitoring) implementable before water availability. Two-year operational testing period to determine optimal add-ons configuration. Contingency of US$25 million estimated for needed WTP investments.

identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial environmental risks associated with construction and operations activities. Construction risks include temporary impacts at construction sites. Operational risks include upstream water shortage from abstraction of large quantities from Joun reservoir, sludge handling and disposal, chemical use and storage, operator exposure to fugitive chemicals and air emissions, noise, and accidents. Exacerbated risk of unexploded ordnance (UXOs) presence near worksites due to Middle East conflict.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Proper implementation of Environmental, Health and Safety (EHS) mitigation measures following risk management hierarchy. Strong ESHS site monitoring and supervision. Proper design and good operational practices including continuous monitoring. Operational arrangements ensuring yearly water allocation of 3 cubic meters per second to Wardanieh WTP without affecting upstream uses. Adherence to EHS requirements and emergency response plans. Before commencement of civil works, all worksites inspected by competent agency for UXOs with removal if necessary and official declaration of safety before workers allowed on-site. E&S specialists hired by both PMUs for overall Project oversight. E&S site supervision conducted as part of engineering supervision contracts. Quarterly progress reports on ESCP implementation. Potential extension of HEIS for E&S support.

identified_risks.risk_description: Social risks and impacts are moderate due to temporary, predictable, reversible nature of impacts, limited social conflict, medium probability of serious adverse effects to worker/community health and safety, and medium magnitude/spatial extent. Risk of Sexual Exploitation and Abuse/Sexual Harassment (SEA/SH) associated with labor influx. Potential risk of ineffective communication and consultation with affected communities. Temporary traffic disruptions and nuisances during tunnel and pipeline works. Land expropriation and resettlement for reservoirs and infrastructure.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Workers recommended to be hired from surrounding local communities with close supervision oversight, similar to GBWSP arrangement. No SEA/SH cases reported during GBWSP implementation. Inclusive stakeholder engagement throughout Project cycle via Stakeholder Engagement Plan. Well-communicated temporary traffic disruptions to affected communities. Robust and widely disseminated Project grievance mechanism. Resettlement Action Plan and addendum prepared and disclosed. Expropriation process monitored in line with ESS5 principles. Citizen engagement focus as part of all components with participatory planning, decision-making, appraisal, M&E, consultations, and grievance redressal mechanisms. Meaningful consultation and feedback mechanisms including satisfaction surveys and GRM on water supply service provision.

identified_risks.risk_description: Escalation of Middle East conflict in 2024 has severely disrupted essential services, damaged critical infrastructure, and displaced hundreds of thousands of people, creating humanitarian crisis. Works remain suspended and BMLWE operations severely disrupted. As of November 2024, no reports of damage to GBWSP infrastructure, but implementation severely challenged.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Frequent Security Risk Assessments to identify specific risks and establish security levels based on conflict intensity. POM will include protocols to limit movements to essential operations and establish 'no-go' zones based on threat assessments, with evacuation plans and clear action triggers. Project includes CERC component to allow flexible response to emergencies. No works will commence until conflict has ended and sites are safe for contractors and workers to resume operations.

identified_risks.risk_description: LRA manages sluices at upstream hydropower plants from which water will be diverted to Wardanieh WTP. While 3 cubic meters per second during dry season (50 million cubic meters for 6 months) is protected by Presidential Decree No. 14522 of 1970, plant is expected to operate year-round with no legally binding document guaranteeing additional water allocation during wet season. Risk that LRA could allocate water for other purposes despite water abundance during wet season.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Legal covenant in loan agreement to ensure signing of Cooperation Agreement between LRA and BMLWE. Cooperation Agreement will define water allocation, operational procedures, and compensation mechanisms to ensure year-round plant operation. Water balance conducted during preparation confirmed sufficient water availability for potable water use year-round.

triggered_safeguard_policies: ESS 1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
ESS 3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management
ESS 5: Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement
ESS 6: Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources
ESS 10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Project-level grievance mechanism established with robust and widely disseminated GRM for water supply service provision. Communities and individuals can submit complaints to project-level GRM or World Bank's Grievance Redress Service (GRS). Independent Accountability Mechanism (AM) available including Inspection Panel and Dispute Resolution Service. GRM to be elaborated in Stakeholder Engagement Plan with meaningful consultation and feedback mechanisms including satisfaction surveys. Digitalized call center to track and resolve cases.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: HR survey conducted at BMLWE using Bank Equal Aqua platform revealed women significantly underrepresented in technical roles (only 14.3% in engineering positions), though female representation exceeds MENA average but below global level. Project includes focused training and capacity-building activities for women to enhance skills for technical positions. Component 5 supports apprenticeship and certification program targeting women with goal of 50% female participation (60 apprentices total, 30 female target). Program provides one-year apprenticeship for recent graduates and final-year students with certifications endorsed by partnering universities. Project will strengthen BMLWE's institutional capacity to sustain programs beyond duration and assist in reviewing HR policies to promote women's participation. PDO indicator includes female beneficiaries (919,800 women of 1.8 million total beneficiaries). Social specialist will coordinate on gender activities.
vulnerable_groups: Internally displaced persons from southern Lebanon displaced by Middle East conflict
Syrian refugees (over 318,000 registered in Beqaa Valley, many in informal settlements)
Low-income households currently relying on expensive water tankers (US$4-6/m3 vs BMLWE US$0.5/m3)
Women in technical positions at BMLWE
Youth (ages 15-24) in project areas (16.7% of population)
Project Affected Persons (PAPs) requiring resettlement for land acquisition

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed to address fragility and conflict context. Recognizes that Lebanon's ongoing multi-faceted crisis has deepened urgency of water security. Project includes provisions for repairs and rehabilitation of infrastructure potentially damaged by Middle East conflict. CERC component (zero allocation at onset) allows flexible response to emergencies. Security Risk Assessments to be conducted frequently with protocols for limiting movements to essential operations and establishing 'no-go' zones. Evacuation plans with clear triggers included in POM. No works will commence until conflict has ended and sites are safe. Project supports water sector reform as part of government's recovery agenda. Strong citizen engagement focus to build trust and social cohesion. Stakeholder Engagement Plan addresses communication with affected communities. Project contributes to resilience by improving water security and reducing reliance on informal/expensive water markets.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Lebanon experiencing severe multi-faceted fragility: (1) Financial crisis since 2019 with 35% economic contraction, 98% currency devaluation, triple-digit inflation, banking system collapse, dollarized cash-based economy; (2) Political instability with no president since October 2022, government in caretaker capacity, fragmented governance system; (3) Escalation of Middle East conflict in 2024 causing infrastructure damage, displacement of hundreds of thousands, humanitarian crisis, disruption of essential services and government operations; (4) Influx of Syrian refugees (318,000+ in Beqaa Valley) straining resources; (5) Collapse of public services including water, electricity, healthcare; (6) High unemployment and poverty; (7) Weak institutional capacity with high staff turnover in public sector; (8) Widespread corruption perception; (9) Deteriorating water quality in Litani River basin from untreated wastewater and agricultural runoff; (10) Climate change impacts including rainfall seasonality and drought risk.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Escalation of Middle East conflict in 2024 has severely disrupted essential services, damaged critical infrastructure, and displaced hundreds of thousands. As of November 2024, no reports of damage to GBWSP infrastructure but works remain suspended and BMLWE operations severely disrupted. Exacerbated risk of unexploded ordnance (UXOs) near worksites. Project includes frequent Security Risk Assessments to identify specific risks and establish security levels based on conflict intensity. POM will include protocols to limit movements to essential operations and establish 'no-go' zones based on threat assessments with evacuation plans and clear action triggers. No works will commence until conflict has ended and sites are safe for contractors and workers. Potential increase in number of unarmed security guards at Project sites. CERC component allows flexible response to security emergencies.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - Multiple displacement and refugee issues: (1) Displacement of hundreds of thousands of people from southern Lebanon to Beirut driven by Middle East conflict escalation in 2024, expected to further strain BMLWE's capacity to meet growing water demand; (2) Over 318,000 Syrian refugees registered in Beqaa Valley, many living in informal settlements lacking proper sewage connections and relying on unsustainable agricultural practices, contributing to water quality deterioration in Litani River basin; (3) Project-related displacement: Land acquisition and resettlement compensation required for reservoirs (Hadath 90, Hazmieh 90), twin transmission pipelines section, Joun Regulation Structure, and Wardanieh WTP outflow. Resettlement Action Plan and addendum prepared and disclosed. Component 4 (US$15 million) finances land acquisition and resettlement compensation for displaced persons. Implementation follows RAP provisions with separate funds flow mechanism for resettlement assistance. Payments made based on actual compensation to Project Affected Persons (PAPs) with receipts confirming receipt. Funds remain available throughout implementation for all notified PAPs. Outstanding compensations at Project closing refunded to Bank.
